analogy: Think of "Tribes Nomads and Settled Communities" through the chapter's own opening images: 79 THE MAKING OF REGIONAL CULTURES Find out how many states have been created in the last 10 years. THE MAKING OF REGIONAL CULTURES One of the commonest ways of describing people is in terms of the language they speak. When we refer to a... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 79 THE MAKING OF, REGIONAL CULTURES, THE MAKING OF, 81 THE MAKING OF. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Tribes Nomads and Settled Communities is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 79 THE MAKING OF REGIONAL CULTURES Find out how many states have been created in the last 10 years.

The text explains that tHE MAKING OF REGIONAL CULTURES One of the commonest ways of describing people is in terms of the language they speak.

The text explains that when we refer to a person as a Tamil or an Oriya, this usually means that he or she speaks Tamil or Oriya and lives in Tamil Nadu or Orissa.

The text explains that we also tend to associate each region with distinctive kinds of food, clothes, poetry, dance, music and painting.

The text explains that sometimes we take these identities for granted and assume that they have existed from time immemorial.

The text explains that however, the frontiers separating regions have evolved over time (and in fact are still changing).

The text explains that also, what we understand as regional cultures today are often the product of complex processes of intermixing of local traditions with ideas from other parts of the subcontinent.

The text explains that as regions, others seem to be similar across regions, and yet others derive from older practices in a particular area, but take a new form in other regions.

The text explains that the Cheras and the Development of Malayalam Let us begin by looking at an example of the connection between language and region.

The text explains that the Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram was established in the ninth century in the south-western part of the peninsula, part of the present-day Kerala.

The text explains that it is likely that Malayalam was spoken in this area.

The text explains that the rulers introduced the Malayalam language and script in their inscriptions.

The text explains that in fact, this is one of the earliest examples the subcontinent. ?

The text explains that find out when the language(s) you speak at home were first used for writing.

The text explains that 1 An early Kerala inscription, composed in Malayalam.

The text explains that at the same time, the Cheras also drew upon Sanskritic traditions.

The text explains that the temple theatre of Kerala, which is traced to this period, borrowed stories Malayalam, dated to about the twelfth century, are directly indebted to Sanskrit.

The text explains that the best example of this process is the cult of Jagannatha (literally, lord of the world, a name for Vishnu) at Puri, Orissa.

The text explains that let us look at the history of one dance form, Kathak now associated with several parts of north India.

The text explains that under the Mughal emperors and their nobles, Kathak was performed in the court, where it acquired its present features and developed into a form of dance with a distinctive style.

The text explains that 6 Kathak dancers, a court painting. “Classical” dances The question of defining any art form as “classical” is often quite complicated.

The text explains that with the decline of the Mughal Empire, many painters moved out to the courts of the emerging regional states (see also Chapter 8).

The text explains that the source of inspiration was the Vaishnavite traditions.

The text explains that from the eighth century, Bengal became the centre of a regional kingdom under the Palas (Chapter 2).

The text explains that between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries, Bengal was ruled by Sultans who were independent of the rulers in Delhi (Chapter 3).

The text explains that also, a wide range of non-Sanskrit words, derived from a variety of sources including tribal languages, Persian, and European languages, have become a part of modern Bengali.

The text explains that early Bengali literature may be divided into two categories – one indebted to Sanskrit and the other Sanskrit epics, the Mangalakavyas (literally auspicious poems, dealing with local deities) and bhakti literature, such as the biographies of Chaitanyadeva, the leader of the Vaishnava bhakti movement (Chapter 6).

The text explains that we have seen (Chapter 2) that temples and other religious structures were often built by individuals or groups who were becoming powerful – to both demonstrate their power and proclaim their piety.

The text explains that the temples began to copy the double-roofed ( ) or four-roofed ( ) structure of the thatched huts.

The text explains that in the comparatively more complex four-roofed structure, four triangular roofs placed on the four walls move up to converge on a curved line or a point.

The text explains that the interior was relatively plain, but the outer walls of many temples were decorated with paintings, ornamental tiles or terracotta tablets.

The text explains that compare the temple shown here with that in Chapter 2. ?

The text explains that 12 (right) A four-roofed temple with a tower.

The text explains that what are the important architectural features of the temples of Bengal?

The text explains that describe the most important features of the culture of your region, focusing on buildings, performing arts and painting.

The text explains that 93 THE MAKING OF REGIONAL CULTURES Let’s discuss 5.

The text explains that why did minstrels proclaim the achievements of heroes?

The text explains that why do we know much more about the cultural practices of rulers than about those of ordinary people?

The text explains that why did conquerors try to control the temple of Jagannatha at Puri?

The text explains that do you use different languages for (a) speaking, (b) reading, (c) writing?

The text explains that find out about one major composition in language that you use and discuss 11.

The text explains that choose one state each from north, west, south, east and central India.

The text explains that for each of these, prepare a list of foods that are commonly consumed, highlighting any differences and similarities that you notice. regions and prepare a list of clothes that are generally worn by women and men in each.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 7: Tribes Nomads and Settled Communities

prelims_traps: Do not treat 79 THE MAKING OF and REGIONAL CULTURES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Tribes Nomads and Settled Communities; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
